title: Effects of Grapes on Psoriasis. Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory debilitating disease characterized by immune system dysregulation and excessive production of pro-inflammatory mediators. This autoimmune disorder results in the release of inflammatory mediators such as T N F alpha, I L-12, and I L-23 and the activation of Th22, Th1, and Th17 from immune cells. These cytokines, especially I L-17, activate the abnormal proliferation of keratinocytes, initiating and exacerbating epidermal hyperplasia and persistent inflammation. While therapies that target T N F alpha, I L-17, and I L-23 have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in treating psoriasis, concerns about the adverse effects, safety, and recurrence of the disease have driven the search for alternative therapies.

sentences.text: Effects of Grapes on Psoriasis.
Psoriasis is a chronic inflammatory debilitating disease characterized by immune system dysregulation and excessive production of pro-inflammatory mediators.
This autoimmune disorder results in the release of inflammatory mediators such as T N F alpha, I L-12, and I L-23 and the activation of Th22, Th1, and Th17 from immune cells.
These cytokines, especially I L-17, activate the abnormal proliferation of keratinocytes, initiating and exacerbating epidermal hyperplasia and persistent inflammation.

While therapies that target T N F alpha, I L-17, and I L-23 have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in treating psoriasis, concerns about the adverse effects, safety, and recurrence of the disease have driven the search for alternative therapies.
Although limited in quantity, recent research has shown the potential of grape-derived compounds, especially resveratrol and its oligomers, in the management of psoriasis.
One study used pro-inflammatory cytokines to stimulate the proliferation and differentiation of keratinocytes similar to psoriasis.
It was seen that treatment with grape-leaves water extracts significantly inhibited I L-8 secretion and V E G F production in keratinocytes.
Grape extracts reduced inflammation by downregulating N F-kappa B-driven transcription of inflammatory mediators.
Another study on psoriasis-like skin inflammation in mice documented resveratrol's efficacy in lowering inflammation.
It inhibited key inflammatory cytokines such as I L-1 beta, I L-6, and C X C L 8 in keratinocytes activated by imiquimod, an immune response modifier that causes psoriasis-like skin lesions.

Moreover, it was also shown that the regulation of F O X O transcription factors by resveratrol may play a role in angiogenesis and healing psoriasis lesions, which could be further explored for psoriasis treatment.

Research on resveratrol oligomers, such as epsilon-viniferin, ampelopsin C, and vitisin A, extracted from Vitis thunbergii roots, has demonstrated that viniferin exhibits higher deposition in psoriasis-like mouse skin compared to monomeric resveratrol and other oligomers.
It was particularly effective in reducing I L-23 secretion and alleviating psoriasis-like symptoms more effectively than resveratrol.
The authors suggested that resveratrol oligomers may provide an enhanced bioavailability and therapeutic potential for psoriasis treatment.
In another study, the extract E F L A 945 from red grapevine leaves was shown to restrict the A I M 2 inflammasome activation in imiquimod-induced psoriasis mouse model.

A I M 2 is highly expressed in psoriatic lesions, and the entry of degraded-self D N A into the inflammasome triggers A I M 2-dependent release of I L-1 beta and I L-18 in keratinocytes, thus aggravating inflammation and skin lesions.

It was found that E F L A 945, which contains resveratrol and peonidin 3-O-glucoside, interfered with the entry of extracellular D N A into the cells, thereby ameliorating the inflammation in psoriatic lesions.

E F L A 945 effectively reduced caspase-1 activation and I L-1 beta maturation in imiquimod-induced psoriasis mouse model, further highlighting the potential of grape-derived compounds in managing psoriasis.
In conclusion, grapes, with their rich array of polyphenols, offer significant benefits for skin health due to their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and immunomodulatory properties.

Grape extracts and polyphenols, including proanthocyanidins, resveratrol, piceatannol, and others, have shown promise in combating skin diseases by protecting the skin from environmental pollutants, promoting collagen synthesis, reducing skin inflammation, and accelerating skin regeneration and healing.
However, efforts are needed for the translation of these findings from the bench to the bedside.
Interestingly, grape constituents are already in use in various cosmetic products, as discussed below.
